9: vehi peh shnayim beruchecha elai: 'Please let there be a double portion of your spirit on me.' Elisha's request for a 'double portion' (pi shnayim) is technically a request for the eldest son's inheritance share (Deuteronomy 21:17) — he is asking to be Elijah's primary heir and successor. Elijah responds that this is a 'hard thing' (kasha) but it will be granted if Elisha sees him taken. Elisha does see it, receives the spirit, and performs miracles that echo and exceed Elijah's. The Elijah-Elisha succession pattern shapes the NT's understanding of John the Baptist-Jesus relationship: John comes in the spirit and power of Elijah (Luke 1:17), and Jesus works in a pattern that exceeds John's, as Elisha's miracles exceeded Elijah's in number (Elijah performs 8 miracles in Kings; Elisha performs 16).
14: Elisha’s cry at the Jordan before striking the water with Elijah’s mantle — ʾayyēh YHWH ʾelōhê ʾēliyyāhû, ‘where is YHWH the God of Elijah?’ — is the prophet’s demand for theophanic demonstration before the act of succession. The question is rhetorical: it is a cry that calls for an answer in action. The waters divide; the prophetic inheritance is confirmed. The same structure — calling on YHWH before a public miracle to demonstrate divine presence and prophetic authorization — appears in Elijah’s Carmel prayer (1 Kings 18:36: ʿăṡeh yadûʿ hayyôm kî ʾattāh ʾelōhîm bᵉyiśrāʾēl, ‘let it be known today that you are God in Israel’) and in Jesus’s prayer before raising Lazarus (John 11:41-42: Pater, eucharistō soi hoti ēkousas mou... hina pisteusōsin hoti sy me apesteilas — ‘Father, I thank you that you have heard me... so that they may believe that you sent me’). In each case the public prayer functions not to inform YHWH but to demonstrate to the watching crowd that the miracle comes from YHWH through his authorized agent. Elisha’s Jordan-strike with the mantle is also the proof-fulfillment of the double-portion request (v9): the same Spirit that worked through Elijah now works through Elisha, demonstrated by the same Jordan sign.
1: wayhî bahăʿălôṯ YHWH ʾeṯ-ʾĒliyyāhû bassᵉʿārāh haššāmāyim — “when YHWH was about to take Elijah up to heaven in the storm-wind.∝ saʿărāh (storm-wind, whirlwind) appears in Job 38:1 and 40:6 as the medium of divine speech; in Ezekiel 1:4 it introduces the divine chariot-throne vision. The same term describes the vehicle of Elijah’s departure, placing his translation in the tradition of theophanic storm-presence. The journey begins at Gilgal — possibly the Gilgal near Bethel, associated with the early prophetic communities.
2: Elijah’s threefold instruction “Stay here∝ (šeḇ-nāʾ pōh) and Elisha’s threefold refusal — ḥay-YHWH wᵉḥay-napšᵉḵā ʾim-ʾeʿezeḇekā (“as YHWH lives and as you yourself live, I will not leave you∝) — is the most direct echo of Ruth 1:16-17 in the prophetic books. Elisha’s oath formula mirrors Ruth’s covenant loyalty to Naomi. The narrative presents Elisha’s persistence as a condition of his prophetic inheritance: he receives by not letting go.
3: wayyēṣᵉʾû ḇᵉnê-hannᵉḇîʾîm ʾăšer-bêṯ-ʾĒl ʾel-ʾĔlîšāʿ wayyōʾmᵉrû ʾēlāyw hayāḏaʿtā kî hayyôm YHWH lōqēaḥ ʾeṯ-ʾăḏōnêḵā mēʿal rōʾšêḵā — “Do you know that today YHWH is taking your master from over your head?∝ The prophetic guild (ḇᵉnê-hannᵉḇîʾîm — sons of the prophets) at Bethel already knows. Elisha’s response — “Yes, I know; be silent∝ — indicates that knowledge of the event does not diminish its weight. The command hᵉḥāšû (be silent) implies grief or reverence.
4: The second stage of the journey — to Jericho — repeats the Bethel pattern exactly. Elijah’s instruction to stay and Elisha’s oath of refusal are verbatim repetitions. The triadic structure (Gilgal → Bethel → Jericho → Jordan) traces a geographically significant path: the three holy sites of early Israelite history moving toward the Jordan, the liminal boundary of the land.
5: The sons of the prophets at Jericho repeat the question to Elisha. His answer is identical to 2 Kings 2:3 — the twice-said “I know it; keep silent∝ marks a growing weight of knowing. The prophetic community’s awareness underscores that Elijah’s translation is not a surprise but a disclosed divine act.
6: The third instruction to stay — this time at the Jordan — receives the same refusal. The YHWH-oath is unchanged: ḥay-YHWH wᵉḥay-napšᵉḵā ʾim-ʾeʿezeḇekā. The three-stage journey is complete; what remains is the crossing of the Jordan and whatever lies beyond it.
7: Fifty sons of the prophets stood watching from a distance (meraḥōq). The community witnesses but does not participate. Their standing at a distance mirrors the posture of worshipers before a theophany — present but separated. The fifty is a round number marking a substantial prophetic community at Jericho, consistent with the prophetic schools Elijah and Elisha led.
8: wayyiqqaḥ ʾĒliyyāhû ʾeṯ-ʾaddartô wayyiglōm wayyakkeh ʾeṯ-hammāyim wayyēḥāṣᵉʾû hēnnāh wᵉhēnnāh — “Elijah took his cloak (adderet), rolled it up, and struck the water; the water divided this way and that.∝ The Jordan parting by Elijah’s mantle echoes Moses parting the Sea of Reeds (Exodus 14:21-22) and Joshua crossing the Jordan (Joshua 3:14-17). Elijah stands in the sequence of the great exodus leaders; his mantle is his prophetic authority made physical.
10: hiqqāšîṯā liš'ōl — “You have asked a hard thing.∝ qāšāh (hard, difficult) is used of the hardening of Pharaoh’s heart and of impossible tasks. The condition Elijah sets — “if you see me taken from you, it will be so for you∝ — is not arbitrary but revelatory: only the one who can see the divine chariot has the spiritual capacity to inherit a double portion of prophetic spirit. The vision is both condition and confirmation.
11: wayhinnēh rekeb-ʾēš wᵉsûsê ʾēš wayyapriḏû bên šᵉnêhem wַיַּעַל ʾĒliyyāhû bassᵉʿārāh haššāmāyim — “behold, a chariot of fire and horses of fire separated the two of them, and Elijah went up to heaven in the whirlwind.∝ The heavenly chariot (rekeb ʾēš) belongs to the same tradition as the divine chariot-throne in Ezekiel 1 and the heavenly armies of 2 Kings 6:17. Elijah is taken up (yaʿal — ascended) rather than dying — one of only two such departures in Hebrew Scripture (the other is Enoch, Genesis 5:24).
12: ʾāḇî ʾāḇî rekeb Yiśrāʾēl ûpārāšāyw — “My father, my father! The chariot of Israel and its horsemen!∝ Elisha’s cry names Elijah as Israel’s true military power — not chariots of iron or cavalry, but the prophet who prays down fire and rain. The same cry will be used at Elisha’s deathbed (2 Kings 13:14), where Joash utters the same words over the dying prophet. It is a title of honor that identifies the prophet as more valuable to Israel than any army. Elisha then tears his own garments (wayyeḥaṣṣēm) — the mourning gesture of radical loss.
13: wayyārem ʾeṯ-ʾaddereṯ ʾĒliyyāhû ʾăšer nāpᵉlāh mēʿālāyw — “he took up the cloak of Elijah that had fallen from him.∝ The mantle falling during the ascent is itself significant: Elijah does not consciously pass it to Elisha but it falls naturally — the succession is divinely ordained rather than humanly arranged. Picking it up is Elisha’s act of claiming the inheritance. The mantle (adderet) is the same garment Elijah used to call Elisha at the plow (1 Kings 19:19).
15: The sons of the prophets at Jericho see Elisha and prostrate before him: wayyiqqadᵉdû lô ʾārṣāh (they bowed to the ground before him). The gesture signals recognition of his succession to Elijah’s authority. Their subsequent offer to search for Elijah — in case “the spirit of YHWH has taken him up and thrown him on some mountain or valley∝ — shows the community still processes the event literally rather than theologically. Elisha’s refusal of the search is his first act of prophetic authority.
16: The sons of the prophets’ offer to send fifty strong men (ḥămiššîm ʾanšê ḥāyil) to search for Elijah reflects their expectation that divine transportation might deposit a living man somewhere. Their persistence despite Elisha’s refusal — pressing him until he was embarrassed (ad-bōš, until shame) — illustrates the difficulty of the prophetic community accepting what they had witnessed. They send the search and return empty-handed.
17: wayyipṣᵉrû-ḇô ʿaḏ-bōš — “they pressed him until he was embarrassed/ashamed.∝ bōš (shame/embarrassment) is used to describe Elisha’s reluctant capitulation to the community’s request. He says “Send” (šilāḥû). Their search confirms what Elisha already knew: Elijah is gone, not merely displaced. The narrative uses their fruitless search to establish the definitive nature of the translation.
18: The searchers return to Jericho after three days. Elisha’s response — “Did I not tell you, do not go?∝ (hălōʾ ʾāmarṯî ʾălêkem ʾal-tēlēḵû) — is a gentle rebuke. The rhetorical question marks his prophetic knowledge as superior to their empirical search; he already knew what three days of searching would confirm.
19: The men of the city (Jericho) report bad water and infertile land (hammāyim rāʿîm wᵉhāʾāreṣ mᵉšakkāleṯ — “the water is bad and the land is miscarrying/causing miscarriage∝). šikkel (cause miscarriage/bereave) describes land that causes premature death of plants and animals. Joshua’s curse on Jericho (Joshua 6:26) included no rebuilding; the bad water may be part of the curse’s residual effect on the site.
20: qᵉḥû lî ṣallāḥaṯ ḥăḏāšāh wᵉśîmû šām melaḥ — “Bring me a new bowl and put salt in it.∝ The new (unused, clean) bowl and salt are ritual objects for a healing/purification act. Salt is used in purification rites (Ezekiel 16:4; Leviticus 2:13); the combination of a new vessel and salt indicates a rite of consecration rather than a merely chemical treatment.
21: wayyēleḵ ʾel-môṣāʾ hammāyim wayyašleḵ-šām melaḥ wayyōʾmar kōh-ʾāmar YHWH rippîʾṯî lamāyim hāʾēlleh — “He went to the spring and threw salt into it and said: Thus says YHWH: I have healed these waters.∝ Elisha performs the prophetic sign-act (throwing salt with a divine word), and the healing is attributed to YHWH’s word rather than to the salt itself. The oracle introduced by the prophetic messenger formula demonstrates that this is authoritative divine action through prophetic mediation.
22: wayyērāpᵉʾû hammāyim ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh kidbar ʾĔlîšāʿ ʾăšer dibber — “The water was healed to this day, according to the word Elisha spoke.∝ The fulfillment formula kidbar ʾĔlîšāʿ (according to Elisha’s word) confirms the healing as prophetically accomplished. “To this day∝ is an archival note indicating that the tradition of the sweet water at Jericho was still told when Kings was composed.
23: wayyaʿal miššām bêṯ-ʾĒl wᵉhûʾ ʿōleh baddereḵ ûnᵉʿārîm qᵉṭannîm yāṣᵉʾû min-hāʿîr wayyiṯqalᵉsû-ḇô wayyōʾmᵉrû lô ʿălēh qērēaḥ ʿălēh qērēaḥ — “young boys came out of the city and mocked him, saying: Go up, baldhead! Go up, baldhead!∝ nᵉʿārîm qᵉṭannîm (small/young boys) are juveniles rather than young men; qērēaḥ (baldhead) was apparently a term of mockery. Whether Elisha was actually bald or this was a general insult is debated; “go up∝ may mock the ascension of Elijah (“you go up too!∝) or be a command to leave Bethel.
24: wayyipᵉn ʾaḥărāyw wayyirʾēm wayyᵉqallᵉlēm bᵉšēm YHWH wayyēṣᵉʾnāh šᵉtayim ḏubbîm min-hayyaʿar wattibbaqʿanāh mēhem arbaʿîm ûšᵉnê yᵉlāḏîm — “he turned around, looked at them, and called down a curse on them in the name of YHWH, and two female bears came out of the forest and mauled forty-two of the boys.∝ qilel (curse) in YHWH’s name is a prophetic act with divine authority. The narrative is disturbing to modern readers; it functions in the narrative as a defense of prophetic honor: mocking YHWH’s prophet is mocking YHWH. The forty-two number is precise enough to be traditional; the two bears (ḏubbîm) from the forest complete the portrait of the land’s danger when prophetic authority is rejected.
25: Elisha goes from Bethel to Mount Carmel — Elijah’s mountain — and then returns to Samaria. The itinerary traces Elisha’s establishment of his prophetic authority across the central hill country. Mount Carmel was associated with Elijah’s great contest with the Baal prophets (1 Kgs 18); Elisha’s visit there is his implicit claim to stand in that tradition.
